perturbation.original_text: 5.1 Agreement for Transfer and Exchange of Information. (a) Each of Parent and SpinCo, on behalf of itself and each member of its Group, acknowledges and agrees that, with respect to Information that it will own as a result of the Separation, each is entitled to physical possession of Information that exists in tangible and intangible form, including Software, Technology, or electronic data that may exist on hard-drives, or other electronic storage means ("Tangible/Intangible Information"). Subject to subsections (i)-(iii) of this Section 5.1(a), each Party agrees that prior to the date that is six (6) months after the Effective Time ("Delivery Date"), it will deliver possession of any Tangible/Intangible Information of the other Party that is in its possession or control to the other Party, without retaining any copies.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 Agreement for Transfer and Exchange of Information. (a) Each of Parent and SpinCo, on behalf of itself and each member of its Group, acknowledges and agrees that, with respect to Information that it will own as a result of the Separation, each is entitled to physical possession of Information that exists in tangible and intangible form, including Software, Technology, or electronic data that may exist on hard-drives, or other electronic storage means ("Tangible/Intangible Information"). Subject to subsections (i)-(iii) of this Section 5.1(a), each Party agrees that prior to the date that is six (6) months after the Effective Time ("Delivery Date"), it will deliver possession of any Tangible/Intangible Information of the other Party that is in its possession or control to the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'without retaining any copies' from the obligation to transfer information, the modified contract now allows each party to retain copies of the other's confidential information, creating the potential for misuse or unauthorized disclosure. This could violate trade secret laws and create ongoing liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Theft of Trade Secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets from misappropriation. Allowing retention of copies opens the door for potential trade secret theft or misuse, violating the DTSA if the information is used to unfairly compete or cause economic harm. The original wording, by mandating the surrendering of all copies, provides a higher level of protection against misappropriation; the removal of that mandate increases the risk.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the explicit prohibition against retaining copies of the transferred information. The Law Explanation states that this omission increases the risk of trade secret theft or misuse. While Scraped Law Snippet 1, regarding the Economic Espionage Act, doesn't directly mandate the destruction of copies, the absence of the 'without retaining any copies' clause weakens the protection against potential misappropriation, creating a plausible scenario for legal challenge under trade secret laws. The omission creates a legal loophole.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Indemnification. Except as otherwise specifically set forth in this Agreement, to the fullest extent permitted by Law, SpinCo shall, and shall cause the other members of the SpinCo Group to, indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Parent Indemnitees from and against any and all Liabilities of the Parent Indemnitees relating to, arising out of or resulting from, directly or indirectly, (a) any SpinCo IP Liability, and (b) any failure of SpinCo, any other member of the SpinCo Group or any other Person to pay, perform or otherwise promptly discharge any SpinCo IP Liabilities in accordance with their terms, whether arising prior to, on or after the Effective Time. Except as otherwise specifically set forth in this Agreement, to the fullest extent permitted by Law, Parent shall, and shall cause the other members of the Parent -14- Group to, indemnify, defend and hold harmless the SpinCo Indemnitees from and against any and all Liabilities of the Parent Indemnitees relating to, arising out of or resulting from, directly or indirectly, (a) any Parent IP Liability, and (b) any failure of Parent, any other member of the Parent Group or any other Person to pay, perform or otherwise promptly discharge any Parent IP Liabilities in accordance with their terms, whether arising prior to, on or after the Effective Time.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Indemnification. Except as otherwise specifically set forth in this Agreement, SpinCo shall, and shall cause the other members of the SpinCo Group to, indemnify, defend and hold harmless the Parent Indemnitees from and against any and all Liabilities of the Parent Indemnitees relating to, arising out of or resulting from, directly or indirectly, (a) any SpinCo IP Liability, and (b) any failure of SpinCo, any other member of the SpinCo Group or any other Person to pay, perform or otherwise promptly discharge any SpinCo IP Liabilities in accordance with their terms, whether arising prior to, on or after the Effective Time. Except as otherwise specifically set forth in this Agreement, Parent shall, and shall cause the other members of the Parent -14- Group to, indemnify, defend and hold harmless the SpinCo Indemnitees from and against any and all Liabilities of the Parent Indemnitees relating to, arising out of or resulting from, directly or indirectly, (a) any Parent IP Liability, and (b) any failure of Parent, any other member of the Parent Group or any other Person to pay, perform or otherwise promptly discharge any Parent IP Liabilities in accordance with their terms, whether arising prior to, on or after the Effective Time.
perturbation.explanation: Removing "to the fullest extent permitted by Law," from the beginning of both indemnification clauses eliminates a key safeguard. Indemnification agreements that are not limited by law can be deemed unenforceable if they violate public policy or statutory limitations on liability. This omission introduces risk by creating a potential for unlimited and therefore unenforceable indemnification obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Enforceability of Indemnification Agreements
perturbation.law_explanation: Indemnification agreements are subject to limitations under common law and statutes. Agreements that attempt to indemnify a party against its own gross negligence or willful misconduct, or that violate statutory limits on liability, may be deemed unenforceable. Removing the phrase 'to the fullest extent permitted by Law' removes a critical limitation that ensures the indemnification provisions remain within legal boundaries. The original wording included a caveat preventing it from violating existing laws; the omission of that caveat introduces legal uncertainty and potential unenforceability.

perturbation.justification: The removal of "to the fullest extent permitted by Law" introduces a contradiction because it eliminates a key safeguard ensuring the indemnification provisions remain within legal boundaries. The Law Explanation explicitly states that this omission introduces legal uncertainty and potential unenforceability, supporting the existence of a contradiction. The perturbation explanation also highlights the risk of unlimited and potentially unenforceable indemnification obligations, strengthening the claim that omitting this phrase creates a legal issue.
